hook: Money that makes money while you sleep.
plain: Earn returns on your initial investment, then earn returns on those returns. The growth accelerates over time without extra effort from you.
analogy: Plant a tree. It drops seeds. Those seeds grow into trees that drop more seeds. Give it decades and you've got a forest from one sapling.
prompt: Pick one subscription you pay monthly. What would happen if you cancelled it today and invested that amount instead for the next 10 years?

hook: Every choice costs you the thing you didn't choose.
plain: Choosing one thing means giving up another. The real cost isn't just what you pay, it's also what you couldn't do instead (also known as opportunity cost).
analogy: Ordering a burger means you can't eat the steak with that same meal. The steak is your opportunity cost.
prompt: You have 3 hours free this Saturday. If you spend it on Project A, what specific result from Project B are you giving up?

hook: Your brain only lets in evidence that agrees with you.
plain: Your brain filters information to match what you already believe — you unconsciously ignore evidence that proves you wrong and notice evidence that proves you right.
analogy: When you buy a red car, suddenly you see red cars everywhere. They were always there — you just notice them now because your brain is primed to.
prompt: Pick your strongest political opinion. Now spend 10 minutes actively searching for the best argument against it. Did you already know those points, or did you avoid them?

hook: Finishing something bad because you already started it.
plain: Sticking with a choice just because you've already invested time, money, or effort. The investment is gone either way (this is called the sunk cost fallacy).
analogy: Finishing a terrible movie because you paid for the ticket. The money is gone either way — but now you're also losing your time.
prompt: Name one project, subscription, or commitment you're keeping alive mainly because of what you've already put into it. What would you do if you discovered it today for the first time?

hook: Borrow someone else's resources to multiply your output.
plain: Use other people's money, time, tools, or audience to achieve more than you could alone. Amplifies wins when it works, amplifies losses when it doesn't.
analogy: Hiring someone for €20/hour to do a task that frees you to earn €100/hour. You just bought time at a discount.
prompt: What's one task you're doing yourself this week that someone else could do for less than your time is worth?

hook: If everyone's doing it, it must be right.
plain: People copy others' behavior, especially when uncertain about what to do. Used constantly in marketing, trends, and peer pressure — often without anyone noticing.
analogy: A restaurant is packed — you assume it's good. An empty one next door makes you doubt it, even if the food is better.
prompt: Next time you're about to buy something online, delete all the reviews and ratings. Would you still want it? What does that tell you?

hook: Discipline drains throughout the day like a battery.
plain: Self-control is a limited resource that depletes with use. Early decisions are sharper. By evening, resisting temptation or making hard calls gets measurably harder.
analogy: Your phone's battery. You start at 100% — every decision drains it. By night, you're running on 10% and making poor calls.
prompt: What's the hardest decision you need to make this week? Block 30 minutes tomorrow morning to make it, before you've spent willpower on anything else.

hook: Every relationship has an invisible scoreboard of who has more leverage.
plain: Who controls resources, information, or status quietly shapes every interaction. The person with less leverage adjusts their behavior, often without realizing it.
analogy: Your landlord can evict you. You can't evict them. That imbalance changes how carefully you word your repair requests, even when you're legally right.
prompt: In your most important work relationship right now, who needs the other person more? How is that affecting what you're willing to say out loud?

hook: Change the wrapper, change the reaction.
plain: Identical information lands differently depending on how it's packaged. The same fact presented two ways can trigger opposite decisions.
analogy: '90% survival rate' vs '10% death rate.' Same surgery. Same odds. One feels safe, one feels terrifying.
prompt: You need someone to take action on something they're avoiding. Write two versions of the same request, one that emphasizes what they'll gain and one that emphasizes what they'll lose. Which one would actually move them?

hook: What you own minus what you owe.
plain: Assets (savings, property, investments) minus liabilities (debts, loans). The real number that measures financial health — not your salary, not your spending.
analogy: You have €10k in the bank, a car worth €15k, and €8k in credit card debt. Your net worth is €17k, not €25k.
prompt: List every asset you own and every debt you owe. Add them up. Is the number higher or lower than you expected, and what's one move you could make this month to shift it?

hook: Acting like there's not enough, even when there is.
plain: Fixating on what's missing so intensely that it warps your decisions. You hoard, protect, or shrink instead of building, even when expansion is the smarter play.
analogy: Eating too fast at a buffet because you're afraid it'll run out — even when there's clearly more than enough for everyone.
prompt: Name the last time you turned down an opportunity because you were afraid of losing what you already had. What would you have done if you believed more was coming?

hook: Reading the room, including your own.
plain: Recognizing and managing emotions in yourself and others effectively. Knowing what someone is feeling, why it matters, and how to respond without making it worse (the technical term: emotional intelligence).
analogy: Your coworker snaps at you in a meeting. Low EQ: you snap back. High EQ: you notice they're stressed about something else and ask if they're okay later.
prompt: Think of someone you're frustrated with right now. What emotion are they probably feeling that has nothing to do with you?

hook: Fixed pie or growing pie?
plain: Zero-sum: one person's win is another's loss, the total stays the same. Win-win: both sides gain because the total grows. Most people treat win-win situations as zero-sum and lose because of it.
analogy: Zero-sum: arm wrestling — one winner, one loser. Win-win: two people building a business together — both eat more because of it.
prompt: You're negotiating something this week. Write down what the other person actually wants. Is there a version where you both get it, or does one of you have to lose?

hook: Your money buys less every year.
plain: Prices rise over time, so each unit of currency loses purchasing power. If your money isn't growing faster than prices are rising, you're getting poorer even as your account balance stays flat.
analogy: A loaf of bread that costs €2 today might cost €3 in 10 years. Same bread — your euro just got weaker.
prompt: Check the interest rate on your savings account right now. Compare it to your country's current inflation rate. Are you actually earning, or quietly losing?

hook: Losing stings twice as hard as winning feels good.
plain: The pain of losing something hits roughly twice as hard as the pleasure of gaining the same thing. This asymmetry drives irrational clinging, risk avoidance, and bad negotiation tactics.
analogy: You're more upset losing your wallet than happy finding one with the same amount inside.
prompt: What's one thing you're holding onto right now mainly because letting it go would feel like a loss, even though keeping it isn't helping you?

hook: Someone does you a small favor. Now you owe them.
plain: When someone gives you something first, you feel obligated to return the favor, even if you never asked for it. The obligation feels automatic, social, almost impossible to ignore.
analogy: A waiter brings you a mint with the check. Tips jump 20%. You didn't want the mint, but now you feel like you owe something back.
prompt: Name someone who's given you help or favors consistently. Have you returned it, or are you quietly carrying the debt?

hook: Big wins require real downside. Small bets stay small.
plain: Safe choices cap your upside. Big potential gains always carry real odds of loss. The skill is calibrating how much risk fits your actual situation, not eliminating risk entirely.
analogy: A government bond pays 2% guaranteed. A startup equity deal could 10x your money or go to zero. Same capital, wildly different stakes.
prompt: Where are you playing it so safe that you've capped your upside, or betting so big you can't afford the loss?

hook: Question every assumption. Rebuild from the ground up.
plain: Strip a problem down to the most basic truths that can't be argued, then build your solution back up from there. You're not copying how it's been done, you're asking if the old way even makes sense (this is called first principles thinking).
analogy: Elon Musk didn't ask 'How do other companies build rockets?' He asked 'What is a rocket, actually? Metal, fuel, electronics. What does each cost?' Then he rebuilt it cheaper.
prompt: Pick one thing you do a certain way because 'that's how it's always been done.' What are the actual underlying truths, and what would change if you rebuilt from there?

hook: Your actions clash with your beliefs. Your brain rewrites the story.
plain: When what you do contradicts what you believe, your mind feels uncomfortable tension (some call this cognitive dissonance). Rather than change the behavior, most people twist the story to make it feel okay.
analogy: A climate scientist flies business class twice a month. The guilt is real, so she tells herself her conferences offset the harm through influence. The flights don't stop.
prompt: Name one place where your behavior quietly contradicts a value you claim to hold. What story have you been telling yourself to smooth it over?

hook: Lines you draw for how people can treat you.
plain: Clear limits on what behavior you'll accept from others, at work or in relationships. Not walls to isolate, but standards that protect your respect for yourself.
analogy: You tell your boss you don't answer emails after 8pm. It's not hostile, it's a property line. Your evening is yours, work ends at the fence.
prompt: Where are you tolerating treatment right now that you'd never accept if you genuinely valued yourself?

hook: Ideas that spread like infections, person to person.
plain: A meme in the original sense: any idea, phrase, or behavior that replicates itself through culture. Memetic means it has that viral, self-copying quality (i.e., it jumps from brain to brain without central control).
analogy: Someone says 'touch grass' as a joke. You repeat it. Your friend repeats it. Two weeks later strangers are saying it online. The phrase is memetic.
prompt: What phrase or belief have you passed along in the last month without stopping to ask if you actually agree with it?

hook: It's small, but it's genuinely not nothing.
plain: A way to say an action has real, measurable effect, however tiny. Used to push back against dismissing something as pointless. Small contributions compound if repeated.
analogy: Donating €5 has non-zero impact on a charity. It's not huge, but it's genuinely not nothing either.
prompt: What small daily action have you been skipping because it 'won't matter,' even though it would have real non-zero impact if you did it for 90 days?

hook: Tiny downside, massive upside. Or massive downside, tiny upside.
plain: When the potential gain and potential loss are wildly unequal. Smart bets cap the downside and leave the upside unlimited. Terrible bets do the reverse.
analogy: Writing a book costs you 200 hours. If it flops, you lose time. If it hits, your career changes forever. That's asymmetric. Betting your house on a 5% return is asymmetric the other way.
prompt: Look at a decision you're weighing right now. If it works, what do you gain? If it fails, what do you actually lose? Are those proportions sane?

hook: Random luck gets rewritten as a tidy cause and effect story.
plain: Humans hate randomness, so we invent clean explanations for messy events (the technical term: narrative fallacy). We force meaning onto coincidence and credit skill where luck did most of the work.
analogy: A fund manager beats the market three years in a row. Investors call him a genius. He believes it too. Year four, he crashes. It was variance, not vision.
prompt: Think of your biggest win in the last two years. How much was skill, and how much was timing or luck you've been quietly taking credit for?

hook: What you display to broadcast who you are.
plain: Actions, purchases, clothes, or speech used to communicate identity, status, or tribe, often without conscious intent. Most luxury exists to signal, not to function better.
analogy: A founder wears a Patagonia vest to every meeting. It signals 'serious, technical, not flashy' to investors. The vest is doing work beyond warmth.
prompt: Look at the three most visible or expensive things you own. What are they actually broadcasting, and who's the audience?

hook: Your advice is worthless if you don't share the downside.
plain: If you recommend something but bear zero consequences when it goes wrong, your opinion is cheap (this has a name: skin in the game). Real credibility requires having something meaningful to lose.
analogy: A fitness influencer sells a diet plan but doesn't follow it himself. Compare that to a coach who eats exactly what she prescribes. One has skin in the game, one is selling risk-free.
prompt: Who's currently advising you on a major decision, and what would they actually lose if that advice destroys your outcome?

hook: Most of your results come from a tiny fraction of your effort.
plain: In nearly every system, a small minority of inputs drives the majority of outputs (also known as the Pareto Principle). Returns are never evenly spread. Find the vital few, protect them ruthlessly.
analogy: You have 30 clients. Five of them generate 80% of your revenue and cause half the stress of the other 25. Fire the 25.
prompt: What 20% of how you spend your day is producing 80% of your actual results, and are you defending that time or letting it get eaten?

hook: Shield someone from consequences and they'll take reckless risk.
plain: When people are insulated from the downside of their actions, they behave more carelessly than if they'd personally pay the price. Banks, insurance, bailouts, all create this dynamic.
analogy: A trader at a bank makes huge bets with depositor money. If he wins, he gets a bonus. If he loses, taxpayers bail out the bank. He has every incentive to gamble.
prompt: Where in your work or home is someone making decisions they wouldn't make if they personally bore the cost of failure?

hook: One impressive trait bleeds over and makes everything else seem better.
plain: When someone excels in one visible area, we unconsciously assume they're great across the board. Attractive people seem smarter, confident speakers seem more competent, successful founders seem wiser.
analogy: A CEO is tall, well-dressed, and speaks smoothly at conferences. Investors assume he's also honest, strategic, and ethical. None of those follow from the first traits.
prompt: Think of someone you admire or trust in one domain. Are you assuming without evidence that they're equally sharp in areas where you've never actually seen them perform?

hook: Beginners are the most confident. Experts know how much they don't know.
plain: People with shallow knowledge overestimate their skill because they don't yet see the full complexity. Real experts, aware of what's still uncertain, tend to hedge and express doubt.
analogy: A guy reads one book on macroeconomics and loudly explains inflation to everyone at dinner. The PhD economist in the room says 'it depends' and stays quiet.
prompt: Pick the topic where you feel most sure of yourself right now. Is that confidence built on depth and repeated failure, or surface-level exposure?

hook: Averages hide reality. The middle value reveals it.
plain: The mean gets dragged by extremes and masks what's typical. The median shows you the middle of the actual distribution, unaffected by outliers.
analogy: Nine people earn €30k, one earns €10 million. The average income is over €1 million. The median? Still €30k. That's the real story.
prompt: Next time someone quotes an average to prove a point, stop and ask: what's the median here, and does their argument survive?

hook: You can steer your life or let it drift. That's agency.
plain: You're making conscious choices about your direction instead of reacting passively to circumstances, other people, or defaults. It's the difference between authoring and being scripted.
analogy: A chess piece gets moved. A chess player decides. In any situation, you're one or the other.
prompt: Name one decision you've been avoiding this week. What would making it yourself, today, look like?

hook: Instead of asking how to succeed, ask how to avoid failure.
plain: Flip the problem. Instead of 'how do I succeed?' ask 'what guarantees failure, and how do I avoid it?' Removing bad outcomes is often clearer and easier than chasing good ones.
analogy: Instead of designing a faster car, ask what kills people in crashes. Fix those things and safety follows naturally.
prompt: Pick one goal you're chasing. Now invert it: what three actions would guarantee failure? Are you doing any of them right now?

hook: Trust and goodwill are your real currency.
plain: The network of relationships and reputation you've built over time. You can spend it to open doors and ask favors, and you can drain it through unreliability or selfishness.
analogy: Every good thing you do for someone deposits trust into a bank. Every time you let someone down, you withdraw it.
prompt: With the person whose opinion matters most to your career right now: are you depositing trust or withdrawing it?

hook: You adapt to everything. Joy fades. So does pain.
plain: After any major change, positive or negative, people return to their emotional baseline. The new car stops thrilling you. The breakup stops crushing you. Your brain normalizes everything.
analogy: The phone that excited you on day one is just your phone three weeks later. Complete adaptation.
prompt: What are you no longer appreciating because you've adapted to it, that you'd desperately miss if it vanished tomorrow?

hook: A rare, unpredictable event that changes everything.
plain: A rare, unpredictable event with massive consequences that everyone explains as obvious once it's happened. Nobody saw it coming. Everyone claims they did afterward (the technical term: black swan).
analogy: The 2008 crash, COVID, the internet's rise. Invisible beforehand. Confidently explained in hindsight by everyone.
prompt: What low-probability, high-impact event would devastate you if it happened tomorrow, that you're doing nothing to prepare for?

hook: How fast can you turn it into cash?
plain: How easily an asset converts to cash without losing value. Cash is fully liquid. A house takes weeks to sell and costs you money in the process.
analogy: Cash in your wallet is instant. A house is frozen. You can't sell one bedroom at 3am when you need €500 urgently.
prompt: If you needed €10,000 cash within 48 hours, could you get it? What does your answer reveal about your setup?

hook: Humans instinctively split into us versus them.
plain: The hardwired instinct to form in-groups and treat out-groups with suspicion or hostility. It powers politics, sports loyalty, nationalism, and office cliques, usually without conscious awareness.
analogy: Two groups of football fans who've never met feel contempt purely based on team colors. That's tribalism doing its work.
prompt: Where are you treating someone as 'other' based purely on group membership, without actually knowing them?

hook: Your best option if you walk away from this deal.
plain: Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement. The stronger your fallback, the more power you hold. Always know yours before entering the room.
analogy: Negotiating salary with one offer is weak. With three competing offers, you can walk away and both sides know it.
prompt: In your most important negotiation right now, what's your BATNA? If you don't have one, how could you create one this week?

hook: The first number warps everything that follows.
plain: Whatever number or idea arrives first sticks in your mind and distorts all subsequent judgment, even when the anchor is arbitrary or fabricated.
analogy: A jacket marked down from €800 to €300 feels like a steal, even if it was never worth €800. The anchor controls your perception.
prompt: What was the first price, salary, or number you heard in an important situation, and has that anchor been quietly shaping your thinking since?

hook: Tiny daily actions compound into massive results.
plain: Like compound interest but for habits. Small improvements or declines, repeated consistently, produce enormous outcomes over time in either direction.
analogy: Ten pages a day feels trivial. Over a year it's 18 books. Over a decade, you've read more than most living humans.
prompt: What small action, done daily for one year, would create the biggest shift in your life?

hook: Show me how someone gets paid and I'll predict their behavior.
plain: People do what they're rewarded for. To understand why someone acts a certain way, find out what they're incentivized financially, socially, or emotionally to do.
analogy: A car salesman pushes the most expensive model not because it's best for you, but because his commission depends on it.
prompt: Think of one person whose behavior frustrates you. What invisible incentive might be driving them that you haven't considered?

hook: The middle collapses. Extremes harden. Nuance dies.
plain: A process where groups split into two opposing camps with no middle ground. Each side becomes more extreme as the gap widens, and moderates vanish or pick a side.
analogy: A workplace argument that started mild now has half the team refusing to speak to the other half. The nuanced middle collapsed completely.
prompt: Where is a situation in your life becoming polarized that could still be defused if someone stepped into the middle today?

hook: Your brain maxes out faster than you think.
plain: The total mental effort being used at any moment. Too many things competing for attention simultaneously degrades decisions, memory, and clarity.
analogy: Trying to remember a phone number while getting directions and your kid is screaming. You drop all three tasks.
prompt: Where is your mental load heaviest right now, and what single thing could you remove to create real breathing room?

hook: Resist the cookie now. Get the whole cake later.
plain: The ability to resist short-term pleasure for a better long-term payoff. Strongly linked to financial stability, health, and career success.
analogy: The marshmallow test: kids who waited 15 minutes for a second marshmallow had measurably better life outcomes decades later.
prompt: Where are you choosing immediate comfort over a future gain, and is the short-term hit actually worth what you're trading away?

hook: Whoever frames the story first usually wins the argument.
plain: The ability to shape how events, people, or ideas are perceived through the story you tell about them. Politicians and brands do this professionally, but everyone competes to control how they're understood.
analogy: Two people describe the same argument to a mutual friend. Each version sounds like they're the reasonable one. Whoever tells it first and most convincingly often wins, regardless of what actually happened.
prompt: Think of the last time someone misrepresented you. Did you let their version stand, or did you tell your own story? What would you say differently if you got a do-over?

hook: Everything tends toward disorder without energy.
plain: A physics concept used broadly: systems naturally decay and become chaotic unless effort is continuously put in to maintain them. Your body, your business, your friendships all follow this pattern.
analogy: Leave a garden alone for a year and it becomes overgrown chaos. Relationships work the same way: neglect them and they fall apart without any dramatic event.
prompt: Name one relationship or system in your life that's slowly decaying through neglect. What's the smallest action you could take this week to reverse it?

hook: Extreme results rarely repeat. Average always comes back.
plain: After an unusually good or bad event, outcomes tend to drift back toward the average over time. Mistaking temporary extremes for permanent trends is a common and costly error.
analogy: A basketball player who had the best game of his career will likely perform more average next game — not because he got worse, but because the peak was a statistical outlier.
prompt: What recent extreme are you treating like a permanent trend? A great week at work, a terrible argument, a sudden windfall?

hook: One side knows more. That asymmetry is the real leverage.
plain: When one party in a deal or relationship has significantly more knowledge than the other, they have a structural advantage that shapes the outcome in their favor. The gap itself is power.
analogy: A mechanic knows exactly what's wrong with your car. You don't. That knowledge gap is precisely why you might be overcharged.
prompt: In your most important current negotiation or decision, who has more information than you? Name one question you could ask this week to close that gap.

hook: See the whole machine, not just the broken part.
plain: Looking at how all parts of a situation interact rather than focusing on one element in isolation (also known as systems thinking). The solution to a problem is often found elsewhere in the system, not where the symptom appears.
analogy: A city adds more roads to reduce traffic. Traffic gets worse because more roads attract more drivers. You have to see the whole machine, not just the broken part.
prompt: Pick a problem you're currently trying to solve. What part of the wider system might be causing it that you haven't looked at yet?

hook: The failures disappeared quietly. You only see the winners.
plain: When we only see the success stories and draw incorrect conclusions about what leads to success, ignoring all the failures we never see because they disappeared quietly (i.e., survivorship bias).
analogy: Entrepreneurs who dropped out of college became billionaires. But we never hear about the thousands who dropped out and quietly went broke doing the exact same thing.
prompt: What advice or strategy are you following based mainly on visible success stories? Name three people who tried the same thing and failed.

hook: More input eventually stops producing more output.
plain: After a certain point, each additional unit of effort, money, or resource produces less and less gain. Knowing when you've hit this point is critical to efficiency.
analogy: The first coffee sharpens you. The second helps. The fifth makes you jittery and useless. More stops being more and becomes actively harmful.
prompt: Where are you past the point of diminishing returns right now? What would happen if you did less instead of more?

hook: Familiarity feels safe even when it's clearly costing you.
plain: People resist change not because the current situation is objectively good, but because it's familiar (the technical term: status quo bias). Familiarity feels safe even when it's expensive.
analogy: Staying in a job you dislike for years because changing feels risky, even though staying is clearly costing you more in the long run.
prompt: What are you keeping 'as is' right now mainly because change feels uncomfortable rather than because it's actually working?

hook: Everyone assumes someone else will handle it.
plain: People exert less effort in a group than when working alone (some call this social loafing). Everyone assumes someone else will pick up the slack, so collectively, less gets done.
analogy: A tug-of-war team: each person pulls less hard than they would alone. The rope doesn't lie, but nobody feels individually responsible.
prompt: Where are you relying on a group right now and secretly doing less than you would if you were the only one responsible?

hook: Their stake in the outcome makes their opinion biased.
plain: When someone stands to gain financially, socially, or professionally from a particular result, their advice or position is biased whether they admit it or not (this is called a vested interest).
analogy: A real estate agent telling you it's a great time to buy. Maybe true. But they also earn a commission if you do.
prompt: Who are you taking advice from right now who has a personal stake in which direction you choose? What would they lose if you went the other way?

hook: Two choices presented. Ten choices actually available.
plain: Presenting only two options as if they're the only possibilities, when in reality more options exist (this has a name: false dichotomy). Used to trap people into a limited choice that benefits the framer.
analogy: 'You're either with us or against us.' Actually, you could be neutral, partially aligned, undecided — but that nuance gets deliberately erased.
prompt: Where are you stuck between two options that someone else presented when actually there are more paths you haven't considered? Name a third option.

hook: Same facts. Different frame. Completely different emotional outcome.
plain: Deliberately shifting the way you interpret a situation to change its emotional impact on you (i.e., cognitive reframing). A core tool in therapy and resilience: not denial, but perspective.
analogy: 'I lost my job' reframed as 'I've been given time to figure out what I actually want to do next.' Same facts. Completely different emotional trajectory.
prompt: What difficult situation in your life right now could you reframe to find a more useful perspective on it? Write the new interpretation.

hook: Nothing seems to happen. Then suddenly everything does.
plain: Energy or capability that builds invisibly beneath the surface before producing visible results. Most meaningful progress is invisible for a long time before it becomes undeniable.
analogy: Water heating from 0° to 99°C produces no steam. One more degree and everything changes. The work before the breakthrough was never wasted.
prompt: Where are you about to give up because you can't see results yet? What if you're at 99 degrees?

hook: Hard work matters. So do inherited advantages nobody mentions.
plain: The belief that outcomes are purely based on talent and hard work, ignoring structural advantages like inherited wealth, network, geography, timing, and luck (this is called the myth of meritocracy).
analogy: Two runners in a race, but one started 50 meters ahead due to circumstances of birth. Saying 'the winner ran harder' ignores where they started from.
prompt: What advantages did you benefit from that you rarely acknowledge when explaining your own success? Name two specific ones.

hook: Do you believe life happens to you or because of you?
plain: Internal locus: you believe you control your outcomes through your choices. External locus: you believe external forces control your life. Internal is strongly linked to resilience and long-term success.
analogy: Two people get rejected for the same job. One thinks 'I need to improve my interview skills.' The other thinks 'the system is rigged.' Same event — different trajectories.
prompt: In the area of your life where you feel most stuck, are you taking responsibility for what you can control or waiting for external circumstances to change?

hook: The moment the old framework dies and a new one takes over.
plain: The dominant way of seeing something collapses and gets replaced by a fundamentally different model. What was obvious before becomes obsolete. What was heretical becomes common sense.
analogy: Before Copernicus, everyone knew the sun orbited Earth. After his work spread, the entire framework for understanding space flipped permanently.
prompt: What's the dominant assumption in your field that, if proven wrong tomorrow, would make half of current best practices obsolete?

hook: The first thing you see quietly reshapes what you decide next.
plain: An earlier stimulus influences how you respond to a later one, without you noticing. Advertising, interviews, and negotiations use this constantly to steer judgment.
analogy: Researchers who held warm coffee judged a stranger as warmer than those holding iced drinks. The cup primed the judgment before they even spoke.
prompt: You have an important call in two hours. What could you read, listen to, or look at right before it to prime yourself into the right state?

hook: Keep doors open until you have to close them.
plain: Making choices that preserve future flexibility rather than locking yourself in early. The more uncertain the future, the more valuable this becomes.
analogy: An MBA doesn't lock you into finance. It opens ten career paths at once. That flexibility is often the real value, not the coursework.
prompt: Name one decision you're about to make that would permanently close off options. Is the commitment actually worth that cost?

hook: The costs you don't pay but someone else does.
plain: Your actions have consequences for people who weren't part of the decision. Companies privatize profits and externalize costs onto others constantly.
analogy: A factory pollutes the river. Local residents get sick. The company profits. The pollution is a negative externality: someone else pays the real price.
prompt: What cost of a decision you made recently, professional or personal, did someone else end up paying without you fully acknowledging it?

hook: Admitting you might be wrong and meaning it.
plain: Acknowledging the limits of your own knowledge and staying genuinely open to being mistaken (this is called epistemic humility). Rare, uncomfortable, and the foundation of good reasoning.
analogy: A doctor says, 'I'm 80% confident in this diagnosis. Let's run one more test to be sure.' That's not weakness, that's wisdom.
prompt: Pick your most confident belief right now. What evidence would it take to change your mind? If your answer is 'nothing,' you've found the problem.

hook: Consensus becomes more important than the truth.
plain: A group suppresses dissent and critical thinking to maintain harmony and avoid conflict. The result: catastrophically bad decisions nobody individually wanted.
analogy: Every person in the meeting agreed the product was ready to launch. Nobody wanted to be the one to delay it again. It shipped, flopped, and everyone privately knew it would.
prompt: In your team right now, what uncomfortable truth is nobody saying out loud because the social cost of speaking up feels too high?

hook: One honest admission invites another, trust compounds fast.
plain: One person shares something real and uncomfortable. The other feels safe to do the same. A loop starts that deepens relationships faster than anything else.
analogy: You tell a colleague you're genuinely struggling with imposter syndrome. They admit they feel the same. In one conversation, the relationship jumped six months forward.
prompt: What's one honest thing about yourself you've been withholding from someone close that, if you said it out loud, might deepen the relationship?

hook: The watchdog becomes the pet of what it's supposed to guard.
plain: Agencies meant to protect the public gradually become controlled by the industries they're supposed to regulate. The fox doesn't raid the henhouse, it gets hired to guard it.
analogy: A food safety agency staffed almost entirely with former executives from the companies it's inspecting. Who's really being protected?
prompt: In your industry, who's supposed to be the watchdog? Now ask: who funds them, and where did their leadership come from?

hook: The story you tell yourself about who you are becomes who you are.
plain: The internal autobiography we build to make sense of our lives. It shapes beliefs, choices, and self-worth. The part most people miss: it can be consciously rewritten.
analogy: 'I always quit when things get hard' versus 'I take on hard things and figure them out.' Same history, completely different story.
prompt: Write one sentence summarizing the core story you tell about yourself. Now ask: is that story helping you grow, or keeping you small?

hook: Trading some upside to survive the downside.
plain: Taking a second position that offsets risk from the first. You give up some profit potential to insure against catastrophic loss. Used everywhere: investing, business, life.
analogy: You bet heavily on Team A to win the tournament, but place a smaller bet on Team B. If A loses, the B bet softens the blow.
prompt: What's your biggest unhedged risk right now, professional or personal? What's the simplest move that would protect you if it goes wrong?

hook: One small step leads to disaster. Or does it?
plain: The argument that one action will trigger a chain of increasingly extreme outcomes. Sometimes a real risk worth considering. Often a scare tactic to block reasonable change.
analogy: 'If we let employees work remotely two days a week, soon nobody will come in and culture will collapse.' Sometimes true. Often just fear dressed as logic.
prompt: Next time someone uses a slippery slope argument against something you propose, ask: is this a real causal chain, or rhetoric to stop change?

hook: That city you picked at 22 is still deciding your options.
plain: Earlier choices constrain what you can do now, even when the original reason no longer exists. We inherit the logic of the past without questioning it.
analogy: Keyboards still use QWERTY, designed to slow typists down and prevent typewriter jams. The typewriter disappeared decades ago. The layout remains.
prompt: Name one system, habit, or process in your work that exists mainly because of a historical decision that no longer makes sense.

hook: Making someone doubt their own memory to avoid accountability.
plain: A manipulation tactic where someone makes another person question their perception, memory, or sanity. Used to gain control, avoid blame, or rewrite events.
analogy: After saying something cruel in an argument, he calmly insists 'that never happened,' repeating it until she doubts her own clear memory of the conversation.
prompt: Think of a time someone made you feel crazy for accurately remembering something. What did they gain by making you doubt yourself?

hook: Zooming out from the specific to the universal pattern.
plain: Moving from concrete details to general concepts. Useful for spotting patterns that repeat across domains, communicating big ideas, and escaping narrow focus.
analogy: 'This Toyota' becomes 'cars,' then 'vehicles,' then 'transportation,' then 'human mobility.' Each step moves further from the thing, closer to the universal idea.
prompt: Pick a problem you're stuck on. Zoom out two levels: from the specific situation to the type of situation to the underlying pattern. What becomes visible?

hook: The moment slow buildup becomes unstoppable momentum.
plain: The critical threshold where something switches from invisible accumulation to rapid, irreversible spread. Applies to trends, diseases, ideas, and movements.
analogy: A sandpile stays stable as you add grains. Then you add one too many and the whole side collapses. That last grain is the tipping point.
prompt: What small change in your work or life, if you pushed it just 10% further, might be closer to a tipping point than you think?

hook: Build the strongest version of the opposing argument before you attack it.
plain: Instead of weakening an opponent's argument to defeat it easily, you build the strongest possible version of it before you respond. If you can still counter it after that, your position is genuinely solid.
analogy: Before arguing against remote work, you make the absolute best case for it. If you can still counter that version, your argument actually holds water.
prompt: Pick the position you most strongly disagree with right now. Can you articulate it well enough that its proponents would say 'yes, that's exactly what I believe'?

hook: The belief that you won't be punished for speaking up.
plain: In a team or relationship, the belief that it's safe to take risks, say what you actually think, admit mistakes, and ask questions without fear of judgment or retaliation. Without it, everyone just says what's safe.
analogy: The difference between a meeting where everyone stays quiet versus one where a junior employee confidently challenges the CEO and everyone gets smarter.
prompt: In your closest team or relationship, name the last thing someone said that was genuinely risky. If you can't, what does that tell you?

hook: What would have happened if things had gone differently?
plain: Mentally simulating alternate versions of events to learn from mistakes, assess decisions properly, and avoid the bad reasoning of 'it worked out, so it was the right decision.'
analogy: You launched and it succeeded. But what if you'd launched six months earlier or later? Counterfactual thinking forces you to ask that seriously.
prompt: Think of a recent success. What counterfactual factors (luck, timing, someone else's actions) contributed that you haven't fully acknowledged?

hook: Your parents get blamed for your flaws but not credited for your strengths.
plain: A double standard buried in how we think about our upbringing: we externalize the bad (it's their fault) but internalize the good (that's just me). Both actually came from the same source.
analogy: You blame your pushy parents for your perfectionism but never credit them for the ambition and discipline that same pressure gave you.
prompt: What strength do you take full credit for that was actually shaped by the same experiences you blame for your struggles?

hook: You absorb blame for other people's failures to keep the peace.
plain: A pattern, named after the Greek titan, where you compulsively absorb blame for other people's failures in order to maintain harmony, even when it clearly wasn't your fault.
analogy: A project collapses. Six other people dropped the ball. But you're the one lying awake at 2am replaying how you could have prevented it.
prompt: In your closest relationships or team, are you the person who always ends up holding the bag? Who benefits from that arrangement?

hook: Confusing being busy with actually getting results.
plain: The mistake of obsessing over how much effort you put in (inputs) rather than whether that effort is actually producing meaningful results (outputs). You can work 12 hours and move backwards.
analogy: A person who tracks every minute of study time but never checks whether their test scores are actually improving. The metric isn't the mission.
prompt: In your most important current project, are you measuring effort or outcomes? What does the output data actually say?

hook: Advice amplifies who you already are instead of fixing what's broken.
plain: People who most need certain advice are often immune to it. People who already lean in a direction take advice about it to a harmful extreme. Guidance exaggerates predisposition rather than correcting imbalance.
analogy: 'Don't be pushy with women' made anxious men even more timid. The men who were actually steamrolling boundaries ignored it entirely.
prompt: What personal development advice have you taken to an extreme that might actually be reinforcing your existing tendencies rather than correcting them?

hook: Hollowed-out strength that teaches you to be proud of feeling nothing.
plain: Real stoicism is about managing emotions wisely. Toxic stoicism uses emotional shutdown as an identity, mistaking numbness, detachment, and 'I don't care' for strength.
analogy: A man who never cries, never admits fear, never shows love, and calls it discipline. It's not strength. It's a shell where a person used to be.
prompt: Where in your life are you confusing emotional suppression with emotional strength, and what is that suppression actually costing you?

hook: Being sincere in a world that rewards irony.
plain: In an online culture that rewards detachment, sarcasm, and ironic distance, genuinely caring about something and openly showing it is actually the most courageous act available.
analogy: Posting something you genuinely believe, without hedging it with 'but what do I know,' and being willing to stand behind it when people push back.
prompt: What do you actually care deeply about that you've been hedging in public because caring openly felt too exposed or uncool?

hook: Appearing raw and real, but it's all engineered.
plain: When people construct a carefully curated 'no-filter' persona online or in life. The stripped-back, behind-the-scenes look that is actually meticulously designed to appear natural.
analogy: An influencer's 'raw unfiltered moment' that took three hours of setup, 40 takes, and a ring light just out of frame.
prompt: Where are you performing authenticity, presenting a carefully constructed version of 'the real you,' rather than actually being unguarded?

hook: A vague dread that the world is deteriorating and you can't stop it.
plain: The low-level existential unease many people feel about societal direction (decline in institutions, culture wars, technology acceleration) that compounds personal stress without a clear target.
analogy: Doom-scrolling news every morning and feeling vaguely hopeless by 9am, not because anything specific happened to you, but because collective dread seeped in.
prompt: How much of your daily anxiety is about your actual life, and how much is absorbed from a media environment designed to keep you in a state of low-grade alarm?

hook: Chasing happiness is the wrong goal. Meaning is harder but longer lasting.
plain: Happiness is a feeling: temporary and hedonic. Meaning comes from pursuing something bigger than yourself, which often involves discomfort, sacrifice, and delayed reward.
analogy: Raising a child is exhausting, expensive, and stressful, not 'happy' in any simple sense. But parents consistently rate it as the most meaningful thing they've ever done.
prompt: In the areas of your life where you're most pursuing comfort and happiness, are you trading away the possibility of something more meaningful?

hook: Your nervous system becomes addicted to avoiding discomfort.
plain: Modern life is so effective at making us comfortable that the capacity to tolerate difficulty, boredom, or frustration quietly atrophies, making everything harder than it needs to be.
analogy: A person who reaches for their phone every time they feel the slightest boredom or restlessness. The tolerance for even a moment of silence has dropped to near zero.
prompt: What discomfort are you systematically avoiding that, if you faced it regularly, would make you significantly stronger or more capable?

hook: Your ancient brain is running in a modern world it wasn't built for.
plain: Humans evolved for small tribes, scarcity, and physical danger. Most of our psychological problems come from hardwired responses that don't fit modern environments at all.
analogy: Craving sugar and fat made perfect sense when calories were scarce and survival was uncertain. Now the same instinct is driving an obesity epidemic. The software is outdated but still running.
prompt: What instinct or emotional reaction do you have regularly that would have been useful 100,000 years ago but is actively harmful in your current life?

hook: The constant low-grade fear of being judged as unsuccessful.
plain: The chronic worry about how you rank in others' estimation: your career, wealth, appearance, relationships. Named and explored by philosopher Alain de Botton.
analogy: The man who can't enjoy a perfectly good holiday because he's calculating whether his resort is impressive enough to mention when people ask where he went.
prompt: How many of your decisions in the last month were shaped by how they would look to others rather than what you actually wanted?

hook: Opportunities can't find you if nobody knows you exist.
plain: The more you create, ship, and share in public, the more opportunities can find you. Luck isn't about chance, it's about surface area: you expand the number of ways serendipity can strike.
analogy: A chef who only cooks at home will never get discovered. A chef who posts their dishes online, hosts pop-ups, and enters competitions creates a hundred ways for opportunity to walk through the door.
prompt: Name one thing you've been working on privately that you could share publicly this week. What's the smallest, ugliest version you could post?

hook: Almost every opinion you hold is a status play in disguise.
plain: From Will Storr: careers, consumption, political opinions, even social media arguments are driven by the need to raise or defend status within your tribe. We're not exchanging ideas, we're performing rank.
analogy: Two people debate a topic neither has researched. They're not trying to learn anything. They're proving to the room that they belong on the smart side.
prompt: What's one belief you argue for publicly that, if you're brutally honest, exists partly to signal belonging to your tribe rather than because you've actually examined it?

hook: Men have fewer close friendships than any generation before. It's killing them.
plain: Men today report fewer close friendships than any generation before. Social isolation is one of the strongest predictors of early death, right up there with smoking and obesity.
analogy: Women call a friend when something goes wrong. Men scroll their contacts and realize the friendships never got past sports banter and surface-level jokes.
prompt: If something went seriously wrong tomorrow, who would you actually call? If the list is shorter than you want, name one person you could reach out to this week just to talk.

hook: What used to scare you now bores you. That's not progress, it's stalling.
plain: As you grow, yesterday's challenges become today's warm-up. If you don't keep moving the difficulty bar, comfort replaces growth and progress stops cold.
analogy: The lifter who was proud of 60kg now does it on autopilot. If the weight never goes up, neither does strength, no matter how consistent the routine.
prompt: What felt genuinely hard 12 months ago that's easy now? Have you actually increased the difficulty since, or are you just repeating the same comfortable reps?

hook: Design your life or you'll be assigned one.
plain: Building a life around your actual values through conscious decisions, rather than drifting into the job, relationship, habits, and city that just happened to appear.
analogy: Two people live in the same town, same job, same apartment. One chose each piece deliberately. One just never left. Ten years later, their lives look completely different.
prompt: If you wiped away what happened to you and what others expected, what would your life look like if you designed it from scratch starting today?

hook: You lose before you try because the rejection already happened in your head.
plain: An emotional pattern where even small rejections trigger outsized pain, leading you to avoid asking, applying, or attempting. The fear of the no stops you from ever hearing yes.
analogy: He doesn't ask for the promotion because he's already been turned down in his mind. The loss happened before the conversation, so the conversation never happens.
prompt: Where have you pre-rejected yourself this month? Name one thing you decided someone would say no to without actually asking.

hook: Disagreeing without breaking. Rare, valuable, and a flex of real maturity.
plain: The ability to argue with someone while staying genuinely open to being wrong and keeping the relationship intact. Most people confuse debate with combat.
analogy: Two scientists who disagree on a hypothesis don't attack each other — they design experiments. The goal is to find truth, not to be declared right.
prompt: In your last real disagreement, were you genuinely open to being wrong, or were you just collecting ammunition for a position you'd already locked in?

hook: Extreme on both ends. Nothing in the mushy middle.
plain: From Nassim Taleb: instead of moderate everything, operate at two extremes. Very safe in one area, very bold in another. The middle is where mediocrity lives.
analogy: Put 80% of your money in bonds, 20% in moonshots. Or work six focused hours, then fully disconnect. No half-measures, no lukewarm effort.
prompt: Where are you stuck in the moderate middle right now, not safe enough to be protected and not bold enough to break through?

hook: Systems that don't just survive stress. They get stronger from it.
plain: From Nassim Taleb: beyond resilience. Anti-fragile systems improve under pressure. Your immune system, muscles, and hard-won skills all grow stronger through challenge, not despite it.
analogy: Bones don't just survive stress, they grow denser when you load them. Anti-fragility is being the bone, not the glass.
prompt: What stressful situation are you white-knuckling through right now that you could instead mine for the specific strength it's building?

hook: If the app is free, your focus is what's being sold.
plain: The market where platforms compete for your attention because that's what gets monetized. The longer you stay hooked, the more valuable you are to advertisers.
analogy: TikTok and Facebook cost nothing because you're not the customer. Your attention and behavioral data are the inventory they sell.
prompt: Track where your attention went yesterday. How much was directed by your choices versus captured by someone else's algorithm?

hook: Holding two opposite views without your brain catching fire.
plain: The mental skill of entertaining different frameworks and viewpoints at once without needing to resolve the tension immediately. Discomfort with ambiguity is a tell of weak thinking.
analogy: A strong negotiator can hold their position, understand the other side's position, and see the systemic pressure on both without getting defensive about any of them.
prompt: What situation are you seeing from only one angle right now? What would become visible if you genuinely tried on the opposite view for five minutes?

hook: Stop fighting what already happened. That's just adding pain to pain.
plain: From DBT therapy: fully accepting circumstances as they are, not because you agree but because resisting reality adds suffering on top of the original pain. Acceptance frees energy for action.
analogy: You're stuck in traffic for an hour. Raging at the windshield doesn't move the cars. Accepting it lets you listen to something useful instead.
prompt: What reality are you still refusing to accept right now? What would become possible if you accepted it fully and redirected that energy?

hook: Don't chase outcomes. Become the person who does the thing.
plain: From James Clear: instead of setting outcome goals, shift your identity. Behavior follows self-concept. Change who you think you are and the actions become automatic.
analogy: Someone who says 'I'm trying to quit smoking' still sees themselves as a smoker. Someone who says 'I don't smoke' has already changed identity, and behavior follows.
prompt: What habit are you trying to build that you could reframe as an identity? What would you do differently today if you already saw yourself as that person?

hook: Your brain rewires itself based on what you repeatedly do.
plain: The brain's ability to physically change structure throughout life. You're not locked into current patterns. Repeated behaviors, thoughts, and habits literally reshape neural wiring over time.
analogy: London cab drivers who memorized the entire city had measurably larger hippocampuses than average people. The brain changed shape from repeated use.
prompt: What neural pathway are you reinforcing every single day through repetition, and is it one you'd consciously choose if you thought of it as building brain structure?

hook: Imagine it already failed. Now work backwards to figure out why.
plain: Before starting, mentally fast-forward to complete failure and identify what caused it. You build a checklist from anticipated disasters rather than optimistic planning.
analogy: Before launching a product, imagine it's 12 months later and it bombed. You ask: what went wrong? Those answers become your pre-launch priorities.
prompt: Pick something important you're about to start. Imagine it completely failed 12 months from now. What's the single most likely reason, and what can you do about it this week?

hook: The conversation you keep postponing is the one doing the most damage.
plain: Discomfort from a necessary conversation lasts minutes. Avoiding it compounds the damage across months or years. The longer you wait, the higher the cost.
analogy: Telling a friend they hurt you takes 10 awkward minutes. Not telling them costs you the entire friendship as resentment builds quietly over the next year.
prompt: Name the conversation you've been postponing for more than two weeks. What specific damage is that delay causing right now, today?

hook: Your body remembers what your mind tries to forget.
plain: From Bessel van der Kolk: unprocessed trauma doesn't vanish. It gets stored as chronic tension, posture shifts, gut issues, and anxiety that persist long after the original event ends.
analogy: A soldier returns from war with no visible injuries but chronic back pain, digestive problems, and constant hypervigilance. The war ended years ago. His nervous system never got the message.
prompt: What recurring physical tension or symptom do you carry that might not be purely physical, but stored emotion your body never processed?

hook: The product can be average. The offer cannot.
plain: Pile on bonuses, guarantees, specificity, and risk reversal until the offer is irresistible. The product can be average. The offer cannot. People buy offers, not products.
analogy: A gym charging €50/month gets ignored. The same gym offering: membership + personalized nutrition plan + weekly coaching call + full refund if you don't lose 5kg in 30 days. Same gym. Completely different decision.
prompt: Pick something you're selling or pitching. What could you add right now (a guarantee, a bonus, a specific result) to make saying no genuinely difficult?

hook: Value = Dream Outcome × Likelihood ÷ (Time Delay × Effort).
plain: Hormozi's formula: to increase perceived value, maximize the dream outcome and the buyer's belief it will work, while minimizing how long it takes and how hard it is. All four variables matter.
analogy: A weight loss program promising visible results in 2 weeks with minimal meal prep will always outsell one promising results in a year with strict discipline. Same outcome. Radically different equation.
prompt: Pick something you're offering. How could you increase the buyer's belief it will work, or cut the perceived time and effort in half?

hook: Sell to desperate buyers and everything else becomes easier.
plain: Market selection beats product quality. A mediocre offer to a desperate market will always outsell a perfect offer to an indifferent one. Find the starving crowd.
analogy: The best food stall in the world loses to a mediocre one at 2am outside a nightclub. Desperation and timing consistently trump quality.
prompt: Who is your most desperate buyer, the person who would pay almost anything for what you offer? Are you actually selling to them, or to a convenient but lukewarm audience?

hook: Describe their pain better than they can, and they'll trust you to fix it.
plain: When you name someone's exact frustration with precision, they automatically assume you know the solution. Understanding proves expertise faster than credentials ever will.
analogy: A doctor who describes your symptoms before you finish speaking earns your trust instantly. You assume they already know what's wrong and how to fix it.
prompt: Can you describe your ideal customer's exact pain in their own words (the specific frustration they feel at 2am) better than they could describe it themselves?

hook: Carry all the risk yourself and watch conversion skyrocket.
plain: Guarantees shift the psychological burden from buyer to seller. This dramatically increases conversion, especially at higher prices, because the buyer has nothing to lose.
analogy: 'Try it for 30 days. If you're not satisfied, full refund and you keep the bonuses.' The buyer has literally nothing to lose. So why not?
prompt: What guarantee could you offer that would make your product genuinely risk-free for the buyer, and how confident are you actually in what you're selling?

hook: Ship 100 imperfect things. One will hit. Ship one perfect thing. Nothing will.
plain: People over-prepare and under-attempt. Volume of attempts beats precision of a single perfect attempt. Successful people fail faster and iterate more.
analogy: A comedian performing 300 nights a year will be funnier than one spending 300 nights perfecting a single set. Reps consistently beat rehearsal.
prompt: Where are you waiting to be ready or perfect, when shipping something imperfect this week and learning from reality would serve you far better?

hook: Charging more makes your product more valuable, not less attractive.
plain: Raising prices often increases perceived value, customer commitment, and real results. Cheap prices signal a cheap product and attract uncommitted buyers who get poor outcomes.
analogy: A therapist charging €300/hour will be taken far more seriously than one charging €30, even if equally skilled. The price sets the expectation before a word is spoken.
prompt: What are you underpricing out of fear people won't pay more, when the right customers would actually pay significantly more and get better results?

hook: The more specific the problem you solve, the more people will pay.
plain: Generalists compete on price. Specialists command premiums. The more precisely you define who you serve and what exact problem you solve, the less competition you face.
analogy: 'I help businesses grow' = crowded, low-margin market. 'I help D2C supplement brands reduce churn through email sequences' = near-zero competition and dramatically higher fees.
prompt: What is the most specific, painful version of the problem you solve, and are you actually marketing to the people with that exact pain?

hook: Confidence isn't built by affirmations. It's built by evidence.
plain: You don't become confident by telling yourself you're great. You become confident by doing hard things, stacking real proof, and building undeniable evidence that you can deliver.
analogy: You don't become a confident driver by telling yourself 'I'm great at driving.' You become confident by driving thousands of miles in all conditions. The mileage creates the belief.
prompt: What skill would you feel most confident in if you simply put in 10x more reps than you currently have, regardless of talent?

hook: Stop competing. Build something with no direct comparison.
plain: The goal isn't to be the best in an existing category. It's to define a new category where you're the only option (also known as category design). This eliminates price comparison and the race to the bottom.
analogy: Apple didn't compete with existing music players. They created the iPod, iTunes, and an integrated ecosystem. No direct comparison existed, so no price war was possible.
prompt: What unique combination of your specific experience, perspective, and approach creates a category that only you occupy, rather than a crowded market you're trying to win?

hook: People don't want what they're asking for. They want what it gives them.
plain: Customers rarely want your product. They want the outcome your product enables. Identify the real desire underneath the stated desire and sell that instead.
analogy: People don't want a gym membership. They want to look better naked and feel more confident. Airlines don't sell flights. They sell the destination and everything it represents.
prompt: What is your customer's actual dream outcome, not what they're asking for, but what they're hoping that thing will give them?

hook: The person who acts fastest on a good idea usually wins.
plain: Execution velocity matters above almost everything else. Most people have ideas. The differentiator is how fast you move from idea to real-world action and feedback.
analogy: Two people hear the same insight in the same podcast. One tests it Tuesday. The other is still planning it three months later. The first person already has data and is two iterations ahead.
prompt: What idea have you been sitting on for more than two weeks that you could test imperfectly within the next 48 hours?

hook: People overestimate one year and underestimate ten.
plain: Ignore short-term metrics and optimize for compound outcomes. The decisions that look wrong or slow in year one often look obviously brilliant in year five or ten.
analogy: A tree doesn't look like much in its first year. You can't rush the root system. The patience required to build real businesses or real skills is almost always longer than people expect.
prompt: What decision are you about to make for short-term comfort or optics that future you, five years from now, might genuinely regret?

hook: Confidence you practiced feels fake. Confidence you earned through failure is unshakable.
plain: Real confidence comes from surviving hard things, failing, and recovering. Practiced or faked confidence collapses the moment real difficulty shows up.
analogy: An actor who bombs their first audition, recovers, and keeps going earns confidence over 100 auditions. The person who only gets praised and never fails is brittle when the first real challenge arrives.
prompt: Name the skill or area where you feel most confident right now. Can you point to specific failures or hard moments that built it?

hook: Discipline stops feeling hard when it's who you are, not what you force.
plain: Self-discipline isn't a muscle you force. It's an expression of identity. When you genuinely identify as someone disciplined, the actions follow naturally — without the constant internal battle.
analogy: A professional athlete doesn't drag themselves to training every morning. They go because athletes train. The identity eliminates the daily internal debate about whether to show up.
prompt: Pick the habit you struggle with most. What identity shift would make that behavior feel natural instead of forced?

hook: Stop trading hours for money. Package what you know and sell it while you sleep.
plain: Instead of selling your time directly, turn your knowledge into digital products, content, or systems that can earn money without your presence. One piece of work can be sold to thousands.
analogy: A consultant charges €200 per hour for advice. But the same consultant who writes a €97 course sells their advice to 10,000 people simultaneously without showing up.
prompt: What hard-won knowledge or skill do you have that someone else would pay to learn? How could you package that once and sell it repeatedly?

hook: A laptop, a skill, and an audience can outperform a 20-person company.
plain: A single person with expertise, an audience, and a digital product can earn significant income without employees, overhead, or investors. The internet provides leverage that used to require teams.
analogy: A writer with 50,000 email subscribers and a €97 guide can earn more than an agency with 20 employees — with a fraction of the complexity, cost, and stress.
prompt: If you had to build income using only your knowledge and a laptop, what would you create and who would pay for it?

hook: Master something real. Make money from it. Then use it to serve beyond yourself.
plain: Dan Koe's three-stage framework: pursue mastery because you genuinely care, turn that mastery into income, then use your platform and resources to grow beyond your own interests.
analogy: Stage 1: become a genuinely great cook. Stage 2: open a restaurant. Stage 3: train other chefs and build a food culture. Each stage builds on the foundation of the previous one.
prompt: Which stage are you in: still building mastery, earning from it, or serving something larger? What would need to be true to reach the next one?

hook: One hour of work today can keep earning for years without you.
plain: Content, code, and digital products multiply your effort beyond the hour you put in. Unlike physical labor, digital work compounds indefinitely without your presence.
analogy: An article you wrote in 2021 still brings readers to your site in 2025. You worked once. The work kept working — while you slept, traveled, and did other things.
prompt: What skill or knowledge could you package into something digital that would keep working for you long after you created it?

hook: Health, wealth, relationships. Every product traces back to one of these three.
plain: All meaningful products and content ultimately solve one of three timeless human problems (this is called the eternal markets framework). These markets never saturate and always have willing buyers.
analogy: Finance apps, fitness platforms, and dating services are all booming simultaneously. Completely different industries, driven by the exact same three core human needs.
prompt: Whatever you're building or selling right now, which of the three eternal markets does it ultimately serve? Is that the first thing people see?

hook: You are the niche. Your weird combination of interests IS your unfair advantage.
plain: Rather than picking a niche externally, your specific intersection of obsessions creates a category of one that nobody else can replicate. Your unique combination is your brand.
analogy: Nobody else is both a former nightclub DJ and a philosophy-reading self-improvement podcaster. Chris Williamson didn't pick a niche externally — he became one through who he genuinely is.
prompt: List three to five genuine interests you have. What perspective on the world does that specific combination create that almost nobody else has?

hook: The person who can't be fired because nobody hired them in the first place.
plain: Someone who controls their own time, income streams, and location, not dependent on any single employer, system, or geography (also known as the sovereign individual). Made newly possible by the internet and digital work.
analogy: The opposite of someone who can be made redundant with a single email. The sovereign individual can't be fired — because nobody hired them in the first place.
prompt: How many of your current income sources, relationships, and freedoms depend on a single entity? What would it take to diversify that risk?

hook: You keep planting seeds, then digging them up before anything grows.
plain: Abandoning a strategy, business, or skill the moment something new appears, before the original effort has had time to compound into results (some call this shiny object syndrome).
analogy: Planting seeds in a garden, then digging them up after a week because you read about different seeds. Nothing ever grows because nothing was ever given time to root.
prompt: Name a project or skill you abandoned in the past two years. If you'd stuck with it, where might you be now?

hook: You don't know what you think until you try writing it down.
plain: Writing forces you to confront half-formed ideas and expose gaps in your thinking. You discover what you actually know through the process of writing it down.
analogy: You think you understand compound interest completely — until someone asks you to explain it in writing to a 12-year-old. The gaps appear immediately.
prompt: Pick a belief you hold strongly but have never written down in full. Try writing it now and notice where your thinking becomes vague.

hook: Share the messy process, not just the polished result.
plain: Documenting your learning, failures, and process as it happens, rather than only sharing polished success stories (i.e., building in public). Creates authenticity, trust, and an audience growing alongside you.
analogy: A fitness account that shows real workouts, real meals, and real setbacks builds far more trust than one showing only shredded results with no context.
prompt: What are you building or learning right now that you could share publicly in progress, messy and unfinished, that would help someone earlier in the same journey?

hook: Authentic obsession beats manufactured trend-chasing every single time.
plain: Content and businesses built from genuine interest consistently outperform those manufactured to chase trends. Real curiosity produces depth, and depth creates lasting audiences who trust you.
analogy: A person who genuinely geeks out on Roman history for 10 years will produce richer, more interesting content than someone who picks Roman history because it was trending that week.
prompt: What topic do you find yourself reading about and thinking about even when nobody is paying you to? Have you considered building something around that?

hook: Your unreplicable expertise lives at the intersection of your deepest skills.
plain: The specific territory where your skills, experience, and interests converge into genuine depth that others can learn from and value (the technical term: domain of mastery).
analogy: A former nurse who got seriously into fitness has something unique: anatomy knowledge, health science, and real training experience combined. That intersection is harder to compete with than any single element.
prompt: What combination of experiences do you have that almost nobody else has in exactly the same configuration? Who could benefit most from what only that combination produces?

hook: Your past skills don't vanish when you pivot. They compound into the new thing.
plain: When you change direction, you don't start at zero. All your previous skills, experiences, and hard lessons transfer and compound into the new domain in unexpected ways (this has a name: reinvention capital).
analogy: A lawyer who becomes a therapist doesn't lose their legal knowledge. Their ability to analyze complex arguments and spot logical fallacies makes them a more rigorous, effective therapist.
prompt: If you changed direction completely today, what skills from your current life would give you an unexpected advantage in the new path?

hook: Real buyers tell you more in 10 minutes than 6 months of planning ever will.
plain: Sell a rough version before building the polished one. Actual customer reactions beat every internal debate, focus group, and spreadsheet projection combined.
analogy: Rather than spending six months building a full course, offer a live workshop first. If 10 people pay — you've validated the idea. If nobody pays — you saved six months.
prompt: What's the smallest, ugliest version of your idea you could sell to one real person this week? Not test. Sell. Money changes hands.

hook: Hours have a ceiling. Systems don't.
plain: Trading time for money caps you at the number of hours you can work. Building systems that generate value without your presence removes that ceiling entirely.
analogy: A consultant bills $200/hour and works 40 hours. That's $8,000, hard cap. A course creator makes $8,000 while hiking, sleeping, or building the next thing.
prompt: What's one thing you currently do for money that you could document, systematize, or productize so it generates revenue without your direct involvement?

hook: Products expire. Trust compounds.
plain: A product can become obsolete overnight. An audience that trusts you is a durable asset you can redeploy across any offer, pivot, or new direction for years.
analogy: A chef who loses their restaurant still has their following. They can open a pop-up, launch a cookbook, start a YouTube channel. The audience walks with them.
prompt: If your current product disappeared tomorrow, do you have 100 people who trust you enough to buy whatever you built next?

hook: Chase customers, or build something that pulls them to you.
plain: Inbound means people come to you because of content, reputation, or referrals. Outbound means cold outreach and ads. Inbound takes longer to build but scales without your constant effort.
analogy: A restaurant in a busy street with word-of-mouth versus a driver handing out menus door to door. Both work. One eventually runs itself.
prompt: What's one thing you could create this month that would have a customer seek you out six months from now, instead of you chasing them?

hook: Version two built on real feedback beats perfect version one that never ships.
plain: Getting something real into the world and improving from actual reactions consistently beats waiting until it's flawless. Perfection is imaginary. Feedback from reality is priceless.
analogy: Every iPhone ever released had flaws. Apple shipped them anyway. Version 2, informed by millions of real users, is always better than a perfect version 1 that ships never.
prompt: What are you polishing right now that would teach you 10x more if you released it today and improved based on actual reactions?

hook: You're watching their highlight reel with your behind-the-scenes commentary playing.
plain: Comparing your internal experience (doubts, failures, boring Tuesday) to other people's external presentation (wins, milestones, polished posts) is structurally unfair and quietly corrosive.
analogy: You're comparing your blooper reel to their Oscar montage. Of course you feel behind. You're not even watching the same film.
prompt: Name one person whose success regularly makes you feel inadequate. Now list three hard things they've probably dealt with that you never see.

hook: The illusion of free choice inside a system designed to guide you.
plain: Mass media and institutions can shape what people believe in ways that serve those in power, while people feel they arrived at their views independently (i.e., manufactured consent). The choice feels real because the framing was invisible.
analogy: You voted for the candidate you chose. But every news outlet you consumed, every framing of the issues, was shaped by people with specific interests in who wins.
prompt: Pick your strongest political opinion. Now trace exactly where it came from: who told you, what framing, whose interest does it serve?

hook: Four hours of focus beats ten hours of presence.
plain: Three to four hours of genuine deep work (no interruptions, no notifications, full focus) produces more meaningful output than ten hours of fragmented, meeting-interrupted, email-checking effort.
analogy: A surgeon doing three hours of precise work produces more value than an administrative assistant present for eight hours of scattered tasks. Time in the room isn't the metric.
prompt: If you could only work four focused hours tomorrow, what would you ruthlessly cut from your schedule to make those four hours genuinely count?

hook: Favoring your group means disadvantaging everyone else, whether you realize it or not.
plain: People don't just reward those in their group more. They systematically disadvantage outsiders, often without conscious intent. It operates beneath awareness in hiring, friendship, trust.
analogy: Two identical resumes, one with a local name and one with a foreign name. The local name gets significantly more callbacks. No deliberate prejudice required.
prompt: Think of your last three hires, close friendships, or major collaborations. How many came from backgrounds genuinely different from yours?
