plain: Some decisions involve too many variables to process rationally, so experts create shortcut rules that approximate the right answer. Charging at 50 kW means you gain 50 miles per 20 minutes is a heuristic (this is called a heuristic) for navigating electric vehicle charging without doing the math every time. Shortcuts sacrifice precision for usability.
analogy: A doctor treating chest pain doesn't recalculate cardiac risk from scratch. They use the rule: if two of these five symptoms are present, run the full test. Faster, reliable, good enough.
prompt: What recurring complex decision in your work could be compressed into a one-sentence rule that's 90% accurate?

hook: 10% of marketing delivers 95% of the value. You can't predict which 10%.
plain: Most marketing efforts produce modest, linear results. But a tiny fraction, one campaign, one slogan, one product tweak, produces exponential returns that pay for everything else. You can't predict which will be the winner, so optimizing everything for efficiency kills your chance of hitting the outlier.
analogy: In Hollywood, most films lose money. But one blockbuster pays for the 20 flops. If you only greenlit 'safe bets', you'd never make the one that prints billions.
prompt: Look at your last 10 marketing initiatives. Which one delivered results disproportionate to its budget, and did you try to repeat that structure or just move on?

hook: People judge experience against prediction, not absolute quality.
plain: Your brain compares every experience to what you expected beforehand. Ryanair gets better reviews than British Airways not because the service is objectively better, but because expectations are so low that anything acceptable feels like a win. Managing expectations before the experience matters as much as the experience itself.
analogy: The only reason someone avoids Ryanair is the 24 hours of dread beforehand: terror that your bag will be 1mm oversized and you'll get pistol-whipped by Serbian mercenaries. The actual flight? Perfectly fine.
prompt: Where in your customer journey does anxiety or inflated expectations ruin an otherwise decent experience? Reset one prediction this week.

hook: What works for startups destroys value at scale.
plain: Performance marketing, real-time optimization, and personalized ads are brilliant for small companies with limited budgets. When Coca-Cola or Unilever copy that playbook, they abandon what actually works at scale: mass reach, memorability, and brand-building. The tactics that made Shopify successful don't apply to P&G.
analogy: If you're a local landscaper selling hedge-trimming, Facebook ads targeting 'privacy hedge' keywords are magic. If you're Unilever, trying to have a one-on-one conversation with billions of people is insane. You should be buying Super Bowl ads.
prompt: Which digital tactic did you adopt because it seemed sophisticated, even though your business model is closer to a 1970s brand than a 2015 startup?

hook: Calling back a missed call amazes customers. Almost no one does it.
plain: Caller ID has existed for 20 years. Almost no business uses it to call back missed calls. When someone does, it creates disproportionate delight because it's so rare. Zero-cost way to surprise customers, and almost every business ignores it.
analogy: You call a Lotus dealership. No answer. Ten minutes later, they call you back: 'I was outside with a customer and saw I missed your call.' You're stunned. Second time in 20 years a business has done this.
prompt: How many missed calls does your business get per week? Who owns calling them back? If no one does, make it someone's job starting Monday.

hook: What arrives as choice becomes mandatory. Hostility grows when convenience turns compulsory.
plain: A technology enters the market as a convenient alternative to the old way, then gradually becomes the only way. Parking apps started as optional shortcuts but became the only payment method, excluding people without smartphones. The shift from option to obligation alienates users who never consented to full dependence.
analogy: Self-checkout lanes at the supermarket were optional speed lanes. Now entire stores have removed human cashiers, forcing every customer, including the elderly or tech-averse, through the machines.
prompt: Which convenience feature in your product or service has silently become mandatory, and what friction is that creating for a segment of users?

hook: Optimizing for efficiency kills value you never quantified.
plain: When organizations pursue efficiency above all, they systematically destroy benefits that were never captured in a spreadsheet. A ticket machine saves labor costs but eliminates the trust-building moment when a clerk recommends a cheaper fare. The unmeasured value gets discarded because it never appeared in the cost-benefit analysis.
analogy: A railway station replaces ticket clerks with machines to save wages. Passengers lose the clerk who would spot that buying a ticket from the next station saves £15, a service that built loyalty but was invisible to finance.
prompt: What unmeasured benefit does your business provide that a pure efficiency audit would eliminate? Name it out loud to your team this week.

hook: Exploitation funds exploration. Exploration directs what to exploit.
plain: You need two modes: exploitation (squeezing value from what you know works) and exploration (discovering new opportunities). They're not opposites, they're complementary. Exploit gives you cash flow to fund exploration; exploration tells you where to exploit next. Optimizing only for efficiency kills discovery.
analogy: AI researchers understand this instinctively. You can't build a great model by only exploiting known patterns. You need to explore edge cases and anomalies to find breakthroughs. Then you exploit what you learned.
prompt: What percentage of your budget or time last quarter went to exploration versus exploitation? If it was 100% exploit, name one thing you'll explore this month.

hook: Small annoying things fall between stools. Someone should own them.
plain: Most organizations have no one responsible for fixing tiny irritations that fall between departments. The toaster button, the missed callback, the confusing menu create disproportionate value when solved, but no job title covers them. Someone should own the little things that really matter.
analogy: A Lotus dealership calls back a missed call 10 minutes later, the only time in 20 years a business has done that. Caller ID has existed for decades. Who owns that gap? No one. That's why it never gets fixed.
prompt: List three tiny, recurring irritations in your product or service. Who in your org currently owns fixing them? If the answer is 'no one', that's the problem.

hook: Concentrated proximity beats broad distribution. Five neighbors trump fifteen strangers.
plain: People adopt new behaviors through observation of those nearby, not through abstract persuasion. Selling five solar panels to five households on the same street creates more future sales than fifteen scattered installations, because visibility and peer validation drive uptake. Humans are wired to copy what's common in their immediate environment.
analogy: You install electric vehicle chargers in one office building, not ten scattered locations. Six months later, half the building drives EVs because colleagues saw it work and talked about it.
prompt: If you mapped your customer base geographically or socially, where is density highest, and are you leveraging that cluster to drive adoption in adjacent circles?

term: Make It Easy to Say Yes
hook: More ways to buy equals more total buyers. Reducing channels optimizes cost, kills sales.
plain: Customers have different preferences for how they want to purchase. Some want to call, some want to book online, some want to walk in. Each additional channel you offer increases total conversions because you're removing friction for a different segment. Finance teams want to force everyone into the lowest-cost channel, which saves pennies per transaction but loses customers who needed a different path.
analogy: A restaurant removes phone orders and forces all reservations through an app. Tech-savvy diners love it; older customers who would've called and spent more just book elsewhere.
prompt: Which sales or service channel did your business eliminate in the last three years, and which customer segment quietly left with it?

hook: Identical products perform differently based on the badge. Your brain writes the review.
plain: When Toyota and GM built the exact same car on the same production line and badged them differently, the Toyota had fewer customer complaints and higher resale value. Same car, same faults, different perception. Customers expected the Toyota to be reliable, so they interpreted the experience through that lens. Brand perception doesn't just influence purchase, it literally changes how you experience the product.
analogy: You drink wine from a £10 bottle and a £50 bottle. The £50 one tastes better. Later you learn they were the same wine in different bottles, but your memory insists the expensive one was superior.
prompt: If you secretly swapped the branding on your product with a luxury competitor's, how would your customers' experience and complaints change?

hook: One-click default orders eliminate decision fatigue at peak stress.
plain: A panic button is a product feature that saves your most common order and lets you reorder it with zero decisions. Domino's could do this: you open your fridge, find nothing, and hit a button pre-loaded with your favorite pizza. It removes friction at the exact moment decision-making is hardest.
analogy: Amazon Dash buttons for home essentials: one push and toilet paper arrives. The button exists because deciding which toilet paper to buy, every time, is exhausting.
prompt: What's the most repeated decision your customers make? How could you collapse that decision into a single button or saved default they activate under stress?

hook: Rational surprise is an oxymoron. Just do something weird.
plain: You can't engineer surprise through a 56-page PowerPoint deck. Surprise works because it's subjective, arbitrary, and breaks pattern. The AO bear works because a founder decided to keep bears in the van, not because they AB-tested bears vs. aardvarks. Trying to rationalize surprise kills it.
analogy: A PLC trying to innovate: 'We need a framework for surprise definition. Can you prove the bear is better than a stuffed aardvark?' A founder just throws bears in the van.
prompt: What's one thing you could do this week that would surprise your customers, where the only justification is 'it felt right'?

hook: Technical people optimize for function; customers optimize for feeling. The gap is profit.
plain: Engineers and data-driven thinkers assume customers buy the objectively best product. Humans buy things that feel right, look impressive, or signal identity. A Toyota and a Chevrolet built on the same production line have different resale values purely because of brand perception. Engineering can't see this gap, which is why technical founders often undervalue marketing.
analogy: Two watches: one keeps time to within a millisecond, the other loses two minutes a day. The second one costs ten times more because it's a Rolex, and people buy the story, not the accuracy.
prompt: What attribute of your product does your team obsess over that customers barely notice, and what attribute do customers care about that you've never measured?

term: Slow, Slow, Then All at Once
hook: Business uses addition when it needs sigmoid curves. Adoption isn't linear, it's social.
plain: Most business planning assumes linear returns: double the input, double the output. Human adoption follows sigmoid curves, slow at first, explosive in the middle, plateauing at saturation. Products fail because companies expect instant traction and kill them before the social copying phase kicks in. Google Glass was ahead by 15 years; most innovations need patience through the steep part of the S-curve.
analogy: A new product sells five units in month one, seven in month two. Finance kills it for underperformance. Had they waited, month six would have sold 500 as early adopters made it safe for everyone else.
prompt: Which initiative in your business did you or your team abandon during the flat part of the adoption curve before social proof could kick in?

term: Built for the Wild, Living Online
hook: We're biological creatures forced to navigate systems designed by engineers who hate ambiguity.
plain: Human beings are messy, emotional, and analog. But the digital systems we're forced to use every day are designed by people who think in binaries and optimize for efficiency. The result is friction: self-checkout machines that yell 'unidentified object in bagging area,' parking apps that exclude the elderly, hotel showers with no soap. We're being forced to adapt to machines instead of machines adapting to us.
analogy: You're asked to authorize a transaction on your phone, but your phone is in another room charging. The system assumes you carry it everywhere. You just want to finish your work.
prompt: What digital system in your life forces you to behave in a way that feels unnatural, and how would you redesign it to fit human behavior instead?

hook: The best campaigns came from watching how the thing was actually made.
plain: Ad agencies used to send creatives on factory tours, supplier meetings, and retailer conventions. That's where breakthrough ideas came from: seeing how the product was made, who bought it, and why. The Four Springs Butter campaign, one of the most famous in UK advertising, came from someone noticing something during a factory visit. You can't get that from a Zoom briefing.
analogy: A creative sits through three strategy decks and produces generic work. Same creative spends a day on the production floor, notices one weird detail about how butter is churned, and writes a campaign people remember for decades.
prompt: Book a full day in the environment where your product is made, sold, or used. Go this quarter, not next year.

hook: Not knowing when costs more than waiting longer.
plain: People tolerate long waits if they know how long. They can't tolerate uncertainty, even over short periods. Uber didn't win by making cabs faster; it won by showing you exactly where your car is and when it arrives. An insurance company that promises payment in four weeks and delivers in two gets thank-you calls; one that delivers in two weeks with no timeline gets complaints.
analogy: You're stuck in a traffic jam with no information. Twenty minutes feels unbearable. Same jam, but a sign says '15-minute delay due to accident ahead' — suddenly it's tolerable.
prompt: Where in your customer's experience are they waiting without knowing how long? Give them a timeline today, even if the wait stays the same.

hook: Marketing is a worldview, not a department.
plain: Most businesses treat marketing as the team that makes ads. Marketing as a philosophy means understanding that perception, framing, and customer experience create value independent of the physical product. The London Overground was a failing railway until someone rebranded it to sound like the Tube; usage soared with zero infrastructure change. The biggest marketing ideas often come from outside marketing departments because the thinking applies everywhere.
analogy: The London Overground was a failing railway until someone rebranded it to sound like the Tube. Usage soared with zero infrastructure change — pure perception shift.
prompt: If your company stopped all ad spending tomorrow but kept applying marketing thinking to pricing, naming, and onboarding, what's the first thing you'd change?

term: Solve the Fear, Not the Fact
hook: Addressing the worry is often cheaper than fixing the limitation.
plain: When customers express concern about a product limitation, you can address the limitation itself or address the worry. Range anxiety in electric cars can be solved by building bigger batteries or by reducing uncertainty around charging. The second is cheaper and often more effective because the anxiety, not the actual range, stops purchase.
analogy: An insurance company spends millions speeding up claims. Alternatively, they send a letter saying 'expect payment within four weeks' and deliver in two. Complaints stop, costs stay flat.
prompt: Name one customer complaint that's actually a fear or uncertainty. How could you solve it with better communication instead of better engineering?

hook: Most Advanced Yet Acceptable: too far ahead and nobody buys.
plain: Coined by industrial designer Raymond Loewy, MAYA (this is called the MAYA principle) describes the sweet spot where a product is innovative enough to be interesting but familiar enough to be comfortable. Google Glass failed because it was too alien: no social proof, immediate stigma. Ray-Ban Meta glasses succeed because they look like normal glasses, making the tech adoption frictionless.
analogy: A car company designs a concept vehicle with no steering wheel and butterfly doors. Journalists love it, buyers don't. Add back the steering wheel, keep the doors normal, and suddenly it sells.
prompt: What feature in your product is too advanced for your audience to accept right now? How could you phase it in behind something that feels familiar?

hook: Change how you sell, not what you sell.
plain: Commercial innovation means innovating on pricing, presentation, packaging, or distribution without changing the core product. 'Share a Coke' didn't invent a new drink; it put names on cans. Amazon Prime didn't invent new products; it changed how you pay and receive them. Often, these shifts create more value than product R&D.
analogy: Selling shampoo in tiny bottles at airports for triple the per-ounce price isn't product innovation — it's commercial innovation. You're solving a different job: 'I need shampoo but I'm leaving tomorrow and don't want a full bottle'.
prompt: Pick your core product. How could you repackage, reprice, or redistribute it to solve a different customer job without changing what's inside?

hook: Brand equity accumulates like interest; judging it quarterly guarantees underinvestment.
plain: Money spent on brand-building doesn't deliver returns in straight lines; it accumulates. Early investment looks inefficient, but after a threshold, awareness and trust begin multiplying each other. Finance systems judge brand work as if every dollar should produce immediate sales, which systematically starves long-term equity. Short-term thinking rewards the person who inherits the brand you built.
analogy: You invest in reputation for five years while competitors chase quarterly sales. Year six, customers choose you by default because you're the one they trust. Your competitor's lead evaporates overnight.
prompt: If you had to choose between a 10% revenue lift this quarter or building brand equity that pays off in three years, which does your current incentive structure actually reward?

hook: Founder-led companies outperform management-led peers, consistently.
plain: Companies where the founder still runs the business consistently outperform those run by professional managers. Founders have frontline focus (they talk to customers), bias for action (they ship fast), and an owner's mentality (they treat every dollar like their own). PLCs optimize for quarterly EBITDA; founders optimize for long-term customer loyalty.
analogy: Willie Walsh, CEO of British Airways' parent company, used to be a pilot. He spent time in front of the mast. Contrast that with a finance MBA parachuted in from McKinsey — the mental models are completely different.
prompt: If you ran your company like you owned 100% of it with your own money on the line, what's the first decision you'd reverse?

hook: Long-term pays after you're fired.
plain: CMOs know they should invest in brand-building and long-term equity. But they put all their budget into short-term performance marketing because they don't believe they'll be in the role long enough to see the payoff. The system punishes patient thinking, so nobody does it.
analogy: The studio exec gets fired after two bad years. The next exec makes a fortune off films the first one greenlit. The person who plants the tree never eats the fruit.
prompt: What's one brand-building investment you've been avoiding because you won't be around to claim credit when it pays off? What would need to change for you to make it anyway?

hook: People fear missing out more than they value gaining the same thing.
plain: Loss aversion (i.e., the psychological principle that losing something feels worse than gaining the same thing feels good) can be weaponized in positioning. Highlighting what someone won't get if they choose a competitor is often more powerful than listing what they will get if they choose you.
analogy: A USP doesn't just say 'we have X.' It says 'if you don't buy from us, you lose X.' The fear of missing that one feature drives the decision more than the promise of having it.
prompt: State your core offer as a specific loss someone experiences if they don't work with you, not just an absence of gain.

hook: People hate being told what to do; reframe it as a trade and resistance disappears.
plain: An imposition feels like control; an exchange feels like fairness. Governments and companies impose rules that trigger backlash, but if the same rule is presented as 'we're giving you X in return for Y,' compliance rises. The policy doesn't change, only the framing.
analogy: A city introduces 20 mph speed limits and drivers revolt. Reframe it: '20 mph limit, but we're removing all speed bumps.' Same rule, framed as a deal. Acceptance jumps.
prompt: What policy or rule in your organization is currently framed as a mandate that could be reframed as a fair exchange?

hook: Small unexpected details multiply loyalty at low cost.
plain: Easter eggs are surprising, low-cost product details that create disproportionate delight. A toaster with a 'just a little bit more' button doesn't cost much to add, but it makes you want to use that brand forever. These features work because they exceed expectations in a specific, memorable way.
analogy: A Mercedes seat that inflates around corners makes you feel like you're on a mission to Mars. It doesn't make the car faster, but it makes you choose to drive it every single time.
prompt: What's the smallest detail you could add to your product or service that would make someone say 'I wasn't expecting that, that's brilliant'?

hook: Insisting everything make sense in advance restricts you to 15% of possible solutions.
plain: If you demand that every step of a process be logically justified before you take it, you can only explore the part of the solution space that's already obvious. The other 85% of potential solutions require lateral leaps that don't make sense until after you discover them. Penicillin, Viagra, and the theory of evolution were all found backward: discovered, then rationalized.
analogy: A team brainstorms product ideas but rejects anything that doesn't fit the current business model. They miss the breakthrough that would've created a new category because it required a leap that seemed irrational at the time.
prompt: What idea in your business has been rejected because no one could justify it in advance? What would happen if you tested it this month anyway?

hook: Shift payment from upfront cost to shared savings; removes buyer's risk entirely.
plain: Instead of selling a product for a fixed price, provide it for free and charge a percentage of the measurable benefit it creates. Matthew Boulton and James Watt did this in 1775 with steam engines, charging 50% of the coal savings instead of asking mines to buy the machine upfront. Removes the buyer's uncertainty about whether the product will deliver.
analogy: A solar panel company installs panels on your roof at no charge and takes 30% of your reduced electricity bills. You only pay if it works. Zero risk, immediate adoption.
prompt: What product or service in your business could you provide now and charge for only after it delivers measurable value?

hook: More ways to buy means more people buy; forcing one channel kills revenue.
plain: Customers have different preferences for how they want to transact: some want to call, some want to click, some want to walk in. Offering multiple channels increases total sales because it removes friction for each preference type. Finance teams push everyone toward the lowest-cost channel, which saves pennies but loses pounds by excluding customers who need a different path.
analogy: A hotel removes the front desk to save labor, forcing all check-ins through a kiosk. Business travelers love it; elderly guests and non-English speakers leave and book elsewhere.
prompt: Which buying channel did your business eliminate in the name of efficiency, and what customer segment quietly disappeared with it?

hook: The ad platform claims it drove the sale. You were buying anyway.
plain: Digital ad platforms detect when someone saw an ad before buying, then take credit for the sale. But if you visit a Jeep dealership and then see a Jeep ad online, the platform reports it 'drove' the conversion even though you were already in-market. Correlation isn't causation, but the system is built to claim it anyway.
analogy: You decide to buy running shoes, search for them, click an ad, and purchase. The platform reports a conversion and charges the advertiser. But you were buying those shoes with or without the ad.
prompt: Pick your biggest 'converting' ad campaign and ask: if I turned off retargeting for 30 days, how many of these people would buy anyway because they already decided?

hook: Logic traps you on a decent peak. Creativity leaps to a better one.
plain: A local maximum is a decent solution where every logical next step makes things worse. You're stuck because incremental thinking can't find the higher peak. Creativity is the irrational leap that looks wrong but lands you somewhere better (the technical term: escaping a local maximum). If everyone defines the problem the same way, sequential logic will never get you out.
analogy: A company optimizes its checkout flow for years, squeezing out 2% gains each time. Then someone says 'let people buy without creating an account' and conversion doubles overnight.
prompt: Name one process you've optimized to death but is still mediocre. What irrational change would force you off that peak entirely?

hook: You didn't decide to buy it. You saw someone else buy it first.
plain: When asked why you bought something, you'll construct a rational explanation. The real reason is usually invisible to you: you saw a friend buy it, you wanted to fit in, you didn't want to be first. Social copying drives most purchase behavior, but people almost never realize it influenced them. You think you decided; you actually copied.
analogy: You buy an electric car and say it's for fuel savings. But you didn't research EVs until your colleague bought one and it worked fine. The real reason: proof that it was safe to switch.
prompt: Think of your last major purchase. Who owned one first, and would you have seriously considered it if they hadn't?

hook: Charging 30% more beats doubling customers. Margins matter more than growth teams admit.
plain: Getting existing customers to pay 30% more is usually far more profitable than doubling customer count. Volume growth is expensive and often unprofitable at scale because margins are thin. But marketers obsess over acquisition, ignoring the fact that perception and positioning can justify higher prices with zero incremental cost.
analogy: A software company spends $2 million to double users at the current price and loses money. They raise prices 25%, lose 10% of customers, and profit triples.
prompt: Run the math: if you raised prices 30% tomorrow and lost 20% of customers, would you still be more profitable? If yes, what's stopping you from testing it?

hook: Billing by the hour punishes fast, valuable work and rewards slow busywork.
plain: When you bill by the hour, you make more money by taking longer. Speed and insight are punished; time-consuming documentation is rewarded. A lawyer once said he spends three times as long justifying the bill as doing the actual legal work. The best ideas take 10 minutes, but you can't charge for that.
analogy: A consultant solves a client's problem in 15 minutes because she's seen it before. Under hourly billing, she just lost money compared to the junior associate who takes three days researching the same answer.
prompt: If you were paid for outcomes instead of hours starting tomorrow, what's the first task you'd stop doing?

hook: Account people fear going behind the client's back, so they never reach the real decision-maker.
plain: Agency account managers depend on a single client relationship, so they're terrified of approaching anyone else in the organization. They won't pitch the CEO or CFO even when those people control real budgets, because it feels like going behind their contact's back. This paranoia keeps agencies trapped in low-stakes marketing work instead of influencing business strategy.
analogy: An agency has a breakthrough pricing idea that could save the client millions, but the account lead won't present it to the CFO because it would go over the CMO's head. The idea dies in a folder.
prompt: Which senior decision-maker at your client would benefit from your best thinking, but you've never approached them because it feels politically unsafe?
